title: How This Site Is Hosted for ~$12 a Year
excerpt: GitHub Pages handles the hosting for free. Namecheap sells the domain for about $12. That's the whole bill. Here's exactly how it works.
body:
This site costs me roughly $12 a year to run. Not $12 a month — $12 a year. That's the domain name. The hosting is free.

Here's how it works and how you can set it up the same way.

## The Stack

- **GitHub Pages** — free static site hosting, straight from a GitHub repository
- **Namecheap** — domain registrar where I bought `philstricker.com`

That's it. No VPS, no managed hosting plan, no CDN bill, no serverless function credits. GitHub handles the rest.

## GitHub Pages: Free Hosting From Your Repo

If you have a GitHub account, you already have access to GitHub Pages. It serves static files — HTML, CSS, JavaScript, images — directly from a repository. There's no server to manage, no deployment pipeline to configure, and no uptime to worry about. GitHub handles all of that.

To enable it:

1. Create a repository named `yourusername.github.io`
2. Push your site files to the `main` branch
3. Go to **Settings → Pages** in your repo and confirm it's enabled

Your site will be live at `https://yourusername.github.io` within a minute or two. No credit card required.

For a personal site or blog with static content, GitHub Pages covers everything. It supports custom domains, enforces HTTPS automatically, and has generous bandwidth limits that a personal site will never come close to hitting.

## Buying a Domain on Namecheap

The free `yourusername.github.io` URL works fine, but a custom domain makes it feel like yours.

I used [Namecheap](https://www.namecheap.com) because they're consistently one of the cheapest registrars for `.com` domains and their interface is straightforward. No dark patterns trying to upsell you on hosting packages you don't need.

`philstricker.com` runs about $12–14/year. You can often find first-year promo pricing for new domains even cheaper than that.

When you buy the domain, opt out of any hosting add-ons. You don't need them.

## Connecting the Domain to GitHub Pages

This is the part that looks complicated but really isn't. There are two things to configure: DNS records in Namecheap, and a CNAME file in your repository.

### Step 1: Add DNS Records in Namecheap

In your Namecheap dashboard, go to **Domain List → Manage → Advanced DNS** for your domain.

Add these **A records** pointing to GitHub's servers:

| Type | Host | Value |
|------|------|-------|
| A Record | @ | 185.199.108.153 |
| A Record | @ | 185.199.109.153 |
| A Record | @ | 185.199.110.153 |
| A Record | @ | 185.199.111.153 |

Then add a **CNAME record** for the `www` subdomain:

| Type | Host | Value |
|------|------|-------|
| CNAME Record | www | yourusername.github.io. |

Note the trailing dot on the CNAME value — include it.

DNS changes can take a few minutes to a few hours to propagate. Usually it's closer to the fast end.

### Step 2: Add a CNAME File to Your Repo

In the root of your repository, create a file named `CNAME` (no extension) with your domain on a single line:

```
philstricker.com
```

This tells GitHub Pages which custom domain to respond to. Without it, GitHub won't know to route your domain to your repo.

### Step 3: Configure GitHub Pages

In your repo's **Settings → Pages**, under "Custom domain", enter your domain and save. GitHub will run a DNS check and, once it passes, enable HTTPS automatically via Let's Encrypt.

Once the green checkmark appears next to "Enforce HTTPS", you're done. Your site is live at your custom domain with a valid SSL certificate — no manual certificate management required.

## The Full Cost Breakdown

| Item | Cost |
|------|------|
| GitHub Pages hosting | $0/year |
| `philstricker.com` domain (Namecheap) | ~$12/year |
| SSL certificate | $0 (Let's Encrypt via GitHub) |
| Bandwidth | $0 |
| **Total** | **~$12/year** |

For comparison, a basic shared hosting plan typically runs $50–150/year before you add a domain on top. A small VPS starts around $50–100/year. GitHub Pages sidesteps all of that for a static site.

## What GitHub Pages Doesn't Do

Fair to be upfront about the limitations:

- **No server-side code.** No PHP, Node, Python, databases. It's static files only. For a personal site or blog, that's fine — it's actually a feature, not a bug. No server means nothing to patch, nothing to scale, nothing to go down at 3am.
- **No private repositories on the free plan.** Your source code will be public. If that's a dealbreaker, GitHub Pro ($4/month) unlocks Pages for private repos.
- **Soft bandwidth limits.** GitHub recommends sites stay under 1GB of content and 100GB of traffic per month. A personal site or blog will never get close.

For a personal site, the constraints are entirely reasonable. You get professional-grade hosting, a custom domain, and automatic HTTPS for the price of a couple of lunches a year.

## Worth It?

Absolutely. The setup takes maybe 30 minutes end to end, most of which is waiting for DNS to propagate. After that, deploying changes is just `git push`. No FTP, no control panel, no SSH into a server.

If you already have a GitHub account and a static site — or the intent to build one — this is the obvious choice.
